Image annotation, often known as image labelling, is the method of labelling or classifying an image using code, annotation methods, or both, to demonstrate the data attributes that need to be identified. Annotating an image refers to applying metadata to a dataset. Image metadata provides information about the effectiveness of objects [20]. To label images, we used the LabelImg tool. LabelImg is a Python and QT5 cross-platform tool. First, we construct a file called classes.names. This file contains a list of all the labels we used for labelling. For the dataset, we have two labels. Annotate the rectangle box on the object in the image to pick the area and get the coordinates of bounding box.

The process of image labelling or classifying an image using code is known as image annotation. It used to demonstrate the data attributes that need to be identified individual images from the dataset. Annotating an image is a process of adding metadata about the classification object to a dataset. Image metadata provides information about the effectiveness of objects, labels and object location which helps to train the model[20]. To label images, we used the LabelImg tool. For the dataset, we have two labels. Annotate the rectangle box on the object in the image to pick the area and get the coordinates of bounding box.

The process of adding metadata to a dataset is known as annotating a picture.

After detecting the object, the next step is to calculate the classified objects distance from the camera. To calculate the distance few details are required such as the camera's viewing angle (T1), height with respects to the floating surface (h), and tilt angle of the camera (T2). The prototype, as seen in Figure 3, are forming a right angled triangle with a height (h) and T1+T2 as an angle of camera. It helps to calculate the distance (d) by calculating the tan of the angle multiply with height of the camera from the floating surface as described in eq. 1.

Object tracking and object collection remain after classification, object detection, and distance calculation. Aqua drones run on Deep Neural Networks and understand their surroundings. Aqua drone sees the world through camera sensors and performs object tracking and object collection using a set of mathematical equations. A stream of incoming images is analyzed using OpenCV image processing techniques, and the aqua drone is optimally determined which way to go by using bounding boxes. The incoming videos are evaluated using image processing techniques, and the coordinates of the target are identified by the aqua drone, which then follows a path to the targeted object. The mathematical equations function as follows: first, the center of the frame, which is the camera's output, is measured as described in Figure 4. It's called a 'C' and is represented with a blue color described in equation 2. The two thresholds, left threshold and right threshold, are then measured, and named as 'lth' and 'rth' with a red line described in equation 3 and 4 respectively. After receiving the frame's center and threshold, the object's center is measured using the bounding box coordinates given by YOLO. The point is denoted by 'CBB' in a yellow color line. After obtaining these points, the object tracking logic is used to minimize the distance between the object's 'CBB' and the screen's 'C'. In the process of minimizing the distance, instructions are given to the motor driver as 'left and right' and when the aqua drone is in the desired location the 'forward' message is sent to follow the floating trash and places it in the trash net.



Object tracking and object collection remains after classification, object detection, and distance calculation.

After object classification, detection, and distance calculation the next step is object tracking and object collection.

Untitled125.ipynb might be a warning sign.

Here's a good way to name your notebooks:

[𝐃𝐚𝐭𝐞] _[𝐃𝐞𝐬𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧]_[𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐠𝐞]_[𝐍𝐮𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫] .𝐢𝐩𝐲𝐧𝐛

Example: 20220122_Cluster-Baseline_DEV_01.ipynb

The date makes it easier to find when you need to check on the notebook later. The number notes the version- the higher number, the more recent iteration.

This is useful if you're not used to version control yet or are creating a notebook to experiment. It is better to use version control when you get a chance.

How do you name your notebooks?

A myocardial infarction (commonly called a heart attack) is an extremely dangerous condition that happens because of a lack of blood flow to your heart muscle. The lack of blood flow can occur because of many different factors but is usually related to a blockage in one or more of your heart's arteries.

Optimize data pipelines
Selecting transcript lines in this section will navigate to timestamp in the video
- [Lynn] Have you been working with data that's growing in volume and complexity and wondering how you're going to compute against this data? We'll be taking a look at managed Apache Spark clusters on Databricks Azure. We'll look at cluster set-up, different types of notebooks and a number of data workflows. These notebooks will include data processing with common scenarios such as Spark SQL, visualization and machine-learning scenarios with Spark ML, third-party libraries such as TensorFlow and Scikit-learn. We'll also look at a data pipelining and architectural patterns. I'm Lynn Langit. We have lots to cover, so let's get started.

What you should know
Selecting transcript lines in this section will navigate to timestamp in the video
- [Instructor] In order to get the most out of this course, there are some concepts that you should have some familiarity with. These include Core Azure concepts, such as an understand of Azure Storage, such as Blob Storage, Azure Compute, such as working with Virtual Machines, and Azure Security. In addition, it will be helpful if you have an understand of Core Database concepts, in particular, working with the SQL Query Language, and some familiarity with Database Storage. Additionally, we will be working with programming and scripting languages, particularly Python, as well as some other languages, such as R and Scala. We're not going deep into the languages, however, if you have some familiarity, you'll get more out of this course. If you want a refresher on some of these concepts, we have some other course in our library that cover Core Spark Concepts, Azure Storage Deep Dive's, and Azure SQL Querying.

About using cloud services
Selecting transcript lines in this section will navigate to timestamp in the video
- [Instructor] In this course we're going to be working with cloud services. Although you can start with an Azure trial account and that's always a good way to get started. Some of the features that we're going to be looking at do require a premium Azure Databricks account and I'll call those out as we go through this course. Also when you're done studying be sure to turn off and delete unused services so that you're not getting unexpected charges on your Azure bill.







Meet Databricks Apache Spark clusters
Selecting transcript lines in this section will navigate to timestamp in the video
- [Instructor] To get us started working with Azure Databricks let's consider this quote from one of the Databricks company founders, Matei Zaharia. He said, I think that by 2020 most data will be in either public clouds or cloud-like private environments. And the key words here are not only cloud but also most data. The volume of data that we need to work with is growing exponentially because of new methods of collection. Whether it's informational data from our phones or genetic data from our bodily fluids we are having more and more data that is interesting and we need to process. So Matei vision started actually when he was a student back at UC Berkeley in California, and he envisioned distributed computing that could help us to process these volumes of data, and that's called Apache Spark. So when we're thinking about this new world of data we go beyond the typical relational databases and we think in terms of streaming systems, data lakes and data warehouses. And as we'll see in this course Azure Databricks allows us to account for these new types of data needs all across the spectrum. Databricks itself is built on top of the open source Apache Spark Core, and it includes in this implementation integration with Microsoft Azure, auto scaling, multi-language notebooks, built-in machine learning frameworks and integrated security. Now as we start to work with this system of course this is built on the open source Apache Spark distributed compute framework. So this framework not only allows for distributed compute it's designed to process its computation in-memory of the various nodes. And it's designed to work with a distributed file system. Spark itself is an ever growing ecosystem. As of this recording the Spark Core API includes integration with the following programming languages, R, SQL, Python, Scala, and Java. Sitting on top of that are higher level frameworks where we can work with Spark SQL and DataFrames, Streaming, Mllib for Machine Learning and GraphX for Graph Computation.

Business scenarios for Spark
Selecting transcript lines in this section will navigate to timestamp in the video
- [Instructor] So as a working cloud architect, what types of business scenarios have I found that are a best fit for Apache Spark technologies? In a nutshell, those are around distributed compute, and really what's driving it is the volume of data. For example, I've been doing quite a lot of work recently in genomic sequencing and analysis of genomic information. The kinds of tasks that I've used Spark for in these types of workflows included data cleansing, or Extract, Transform, and Load; fast data serving pipelines; scalable complex processing; and distributed machine learning. You can think of Azure Databricks as a set of three components. You have the Databricks tools, services, and optimizations that surround the core open source Apache Spark distribution, and Apache Spark itself provides the distributed computation needed for these intensive workloads, and this sits on top of some sort of file system. Now natively in Databricks, you have the DBFS, or the Databricks file system, which is somewhat similar to HDFS, which is somewhat similar to HDFS, Hadoop File System, upon which Apache Spark was originally built; however, it's been many years since Hadoop was open source, and so there's a great number of enhancements. Interestingly, as an alternative to working with DBFS file systems, when integrated with Azure, there are some new ways that you can work with distributed file systems, given some of the Azure services, such as Azure storage blobs, and many more, as we'll see in this course.

Understand Spark key components
Selecting transcript lines in this section will navigate to timestamp in the video
- Now as mentioned in the introduction to this course, if you're unfamiliar or new to working with Apache Spark, you may want to review some other courses in the library to get some foundational learning. If you have done some work with opensource Spark, you will quickly see how the integration really enhances the capability of Spark's key components, which are shown here. And just to review them, you'll see that the core of Spark is a user program and you can see on the left side you instantiate a Spark Context and then you set up a RDD or a structure which will hold the distributed data. That's a resilient data set. And the RDDs then can work to have operations performed on them in a distributed manner. And some of the examples shown here are a Cassandra table, which is a type of key value database, mapping, filtering, keying by, reducing by key, caching, so on and so forth. And you can see, once the data is loaded into the memory of the worker nodes, as RDDs or some higher level structure, such as data tables or other types of data contexts which can sit on top of RDDs, then you'll see that there is a DAG, or a Directed Acyclic Graph, which is used in scheduling the operations in a distributed fashion. And so much of the optimization is handled not only by Spark, but when you're working with the Databricks distribution, you're working with a more highly optimized version of the opensource Spark. It is interesting. Most of the Apache Spark committers, or many of them, are employees of Databricks but not all of them. So the Spark Core is slightly different in terms of performance from the Databricks product, which builds on the Spark Core. And I know, in my case, I have actually run performance testing on various vendor clouds and in this course, we're focusing on Azure, of course, with Spark Core in contrast to Databricks and I do see performance enhancements with Databricks. It's interesting. So you can see that the RDDs and the DAGScheduler live in the Spark Context, which lives in the Spark application. That interacts with the cluster manager, which then sends the scheduled jobs out to the worker nodes, which use executors and have cached data, again, Spark is designed to work in memory, to execute the various data processing tasks. So it is a rather complex environment and, as we work through some of the examples in this course, it will be beneficial if you get a little bit of background with key Spark operations.

Azure Databricks concepts
Selecting transcript lines in this section will navigate to timestamp in the video
- [Instructor] So the Databricks offering around Apache Spark has three component areas, as I mentioned previously. So let's dive a little bit deeper into them. So, at the top level, you have the Databricks collaborative workspace and that's expressed as Databricks Interactive where you can work with your cluster through a Jupyter-style notebook. So this is a browser-based integration that we'll be working with very frequently in this course. I like to think of it as a smart IVE because it has information about not only the execution, but also visualizations and documentation. There's also a Databricks production layer at this level and there are APIs, which you can work with. The Databricks runtime includes Apache Spark core, also Databricks I/O, and now Databricks Serverless, which we'll be exploring in addition to running Databricks on more traditional cloud-based VMs. And surrounding that is Databricks Enterprise Security, which is more and more an absolute requirement for many of my customers. Now in this course, we're going to look at Databricks running on Azure. Databricks is optimized for AWS, or Azure at the time of this recording. So, the Azure implementation provides you with managed, scalable Apache Spark servers, integrated tooling and examples, and as I mentioned, is optimized to run in the Azure cloud. So understanding a bit more about what this type of solution provides for you, Databricks Azure integration gives you fully managed Spark clusters hosted on Azure, Databricks workspaces for collaboration, with the Databricks notebooks, enterprise security including very important Active Directory integration if needed. And also, very key in this implementation is integration with Azure services such as SQL Data Warehouse, Cosmos DB, Azure Blob Storage, Power BI, and others. And if we look at this architectural diagram, it gives us kind of an all up view of what the Azure Databricks offering looks like. So you can see, kind of like the core Databricks offering, it has a layer around integration for working with people, and applications. And then it's surrounding Spark core. So you can see in the center we have the Databricks workspace, Databricks workflows, the runtime, Databricks I/O, Databricks Serverless, and Databricks Enterprise Security. Surrounding the core, we have our target users, our data scientists, engineers, line of business, and others. Our target applications in the Azure ecosystem. Those are applications such as Deep Learning/ML, streaming, data warehousing, Power BI, and others. And notice at the bottom layer, we have several different file storage options. And this is what I mentioned earlier. It's really interesting, this era of cloud. Now we can go beyond just a single storage choice. And this really can impact performance, cost, scalability. You can see this is designed to work with Azure Blob Store, Azure Data Lake Store, Azure SQL Data Warehouse, Apache Kafka, and Hadoop. So given the fact that Apache Spark is open-source, why would it be a good choice for a customer to select managed Apache Spark and cloud-based, so Azure Databricks in this case. Well I see it, from my architect standpoint, as faster time to value. It's a much quicker setup for prototyping experiments, as you'll see when we do some examples in this course. The included Jupyter-style notebooks really provide a lot of value in terms of not only getting your data scientists up and going, but actually translating their work into usable dataflows that can be deployed by your DevOps people. Intuitive and integrated console, very easy to use, and the Apache Spark core committers work for Databricks, as I mentioned earlier, which results in a very performant implementation. It also has granular and integrated security, which is required for so many of my customers. And to summarize, so that we can understand differences between the offerings of Databricks Azure versus Apache Spark. In the world of compute, if you use Azure Databricks you get managed, distributed compute. In Spark, you get distributed compute, but you will be doing the management. You will be managing your VMs, you'll be setting up the operating systems, you be patching them, it's quite a lot more work. In terms of the environment, if you use Azure Databricks, you'll use Azure managed virtual machines or containers. So they'll be patched, they'll be optimized. If you set up Apache Spark on your own then you have to size and select your virtual machine sizes, your docker containers, and the container management, and so on and so forth. The notebook interface provided by Databricks is a sophisticated Jupyter-style notebook, it's not the same thing as Jupyter, they actually have done many enhancements, and again I'll point those out in this course. For regular Apache Spark implementations you would have to set this up yourself again. The command-line interface Databricks includes CLI, or command-line interface. Of course Spark has its own Spark-submit CLI, but Databricks CLI allows you to automate some of the enterprise applications such as management of security.









iQMS™ - Purpose
Now let us see what is the purpose and what are the constituents of iQMS™.
iQMS™ is One Global Standard for processes enabling excellence across the organisation.
It defines the way of working in TCS.
It is benchmarked with relevant industry standards and models.
It defines ho to execute different types of projects to meet and exceed Customer needs and expectations leading to a delighted customer.
It also defines processes for Support functions such as Internal IS, Administration, Human Resources, Information Resources Centre and so on.


iQMS™ - Constituents​
Let us take a look at the constituents​ of iQMS™.

There are four pillars of iQMS™ that incorporates the collective wisdom of TCS.

Policies
Lifecycle Processes
Procedures
Enablers: Guidelines, Standards, Checklists, Templates


iQMS™ Policies
Let us look at a video on iQMS™ Policies.

Policies are a set of PRINCIPLES to guide decisions and achieve outcomes.

The various policies available in iQMS™are:

Quality Policy
Security Policy
Environmental Sustainability and Occupational Health & Safety Policy
Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) Policy
Privacy Policy


Lifecycle Processes

Process Handbooks cover the Lifecycle processes for each of the project types in TCS. Process Handbooks typically contain the project lifecycle phases, activities, work products/output for different stages of a project.

Process handbooks are available for various project types as listed below:

Development
Application
Operations
Testing
Infrastructure
Support
BPS


Procedures
Let us now understand how specific `activities are to be carried out.

Procedure Manuals list out the set of procedures that govern a specific functional area like project management, administration, delivery excellence and so on. The procedures contain a set of activities, person responsible for that activity and the outputs generated. Examples of Procedure Manuals available in iQMS™.

Project Procedures Manual (PPM)
Operational Risk Management Procedures Manual (ORM PM)
Sub-Contracting Procedures Manual
Admin Procedures Manual
HR Procedures Manual*


Enablers
Enablers help you in executing day to day project activities.

Enabler Type	Examples
Guidelines	Project Planning Guidelines, Design Guidelines, Estimation Guidelines
Standards	Android coding standards, Google Apps Script coding standards, PHP coding standards, .Net coding standards
Checklist	Contract Review checklist, Project Startup checklist, Final Inspection checklist, Security Review checklist
Templates	Software Requirements Specifications template, Product Backlog template, Defect Log template


Importance of iQMS™ Project Work
iQMS™ has processes, tools and practices which guide you through the various aspects of your project.

Some examples of processes are as follows.

Planning and Estimation
Risk Management
Reviews and Testing
Measurement, monitoring and improvement
Customer Feedback Management
You can access iQMS™ Wiki from Ultimatix - https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php/Main_Page





Objectives
Let us begin with the second topic Project Initiation, Planning, Monitoring.

At the end of this topic, you will be able to:

State how to initiate a project
List the activities at project initiation stage
Discuss what is expected when an associate joins a project
Explain why and when should you plan
Describe how do you monitor the project


Initiating a Project
How do we initiate a project?

Let us look at a scenario when Customer awards a new contract to TCS.

Both the parties sign the contract after legal review
The scope is well defined in the contract. The application should be delivered in 6 months
Meeting with customer is conducted to ensure the engagement is in alignment with the goal/product vision
Delivery Partner will create one or more projects in the TCS system (Project Workbench)
You are assigned as the Engagement Owner for this engagement or a Project Manager for one of its projects
You now  initiate the engagement and project activities to ensure readiness for executing the agreed scope


Project Initiation Activities
Let us look at a video on Project Initiation Activities.

Prepare the Project Plan.
Form the team that will deliver the scope in the contract.
Conduct a self-check to ascertain the readiness of the engagement using the Project Start Up or PSU checklist. PSU checklist is used to:
Verify whether all the engagement/project start up requirements are met, for example environment set up, privacy requirements, intellectual property requirements, information security requirements, agile practices implementation plan.
Identify the open resource requirements and workplace requirements
Conduct the Project Start Up (PSU) meeting with relevant stakeholders and support groups. Key stakeholders include Delivery Partner, Engagement Owner, domain and technology experts, representatives from Delivery Excellence Group and other groups like Infrastructure Services, Admin, HR, Talent Development.
Discuss your needs with these stakeholders and obtain their commitment.
Knowledge Officer will share the details of tools, lessons learnt, best practices and experts from engagements with similar business domain and technology stack.
Get the team trained on the required technologies and business domain.
Get the required hardware, software, seating arrangements and any other resources for your team.
Connect with similar engagements and projects to get inputs.
Identify risks associated.
Assess readiness of the engagement/project to commence the activities.
Finalize the standards and templates to be used.
Create the initial product backlog.


Project Initiation Activities - Further Reading
For more details refer to the following.

TCS-iQMS-052 Project Procedures Manual

PP1012 Project Start-Up https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php?title=PP1012_Project_Start-Up
PP1010 Project Initiation https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php?title=PP1010_Project_Initiation
Project Start-Up is done in the Project Workbench in Ultimatix Navigation: Ultimatix -> Delivery & Intellectual Property -> Delivery Management -> Project WorkBench


Expectations on Joining a Project
Play the audio to understand what is expected when an associate joins a project.

Induction into the project gives an overall understanding of the project and prepare for the assigned role in the project.

What is expected when an associate joins a project?

Read the project documents like Induction Manual, Project Plan, and Standards.

Go through the project Knowledge Portal, if any.

Understand responsibilities and the scope of work assigned.

Understand how the project will help the customer in achieving their business objectives.

Undergo trainings as per project plan - iQMSTM business domain, technology and client specific trainings.

At the end of the induction take an Exit test. This could be a quiz, case study, presentation or any other form.


Importance of Planning and Project Plan
In this video, you will learn about the importance of planning and project plan.

Why should you plan?

Planning:

Enables the team to focus on the engagement objectives, product vision and provides direction
Helps to develop team understanding of the milestones, release road map and deliverables
Helps to plan activities and organize resources to meet the objectives

What is a project plan? 

Project plan is a document which serves as the road map for the project team. It covers the objectives, scope of work, product vision, lifecycle process, stakeholders details, infrastructure & tools, schedule, communication protocol, performance & quality metrics.

Planning is done at two levels:

Project Plan - Engagement Strategy and Plan (AESP) for Agile projects / Unified Project Plan (UPP) for non-agile projects
Detailed Project Plan -  Project Schedule/Release or Iteration Plan (for Agile projects)


Project Schedule or Detailed Project Plan 
What is Project Schedule or Detailed Project Plan?

The project schedule is derived from the operational process.
The project schedule describes: The lifecycle phases, The activities that are required to be accomplished, associated resources to perform these activities and its timeframe
The steps involved in creating a project schedule:

Define the project deliverables and milestones,
Identify the activities to be completed for each milestone
Sequence the activities along with start date and end date
Identify activity dependencies, Assign activities to team members


Various Sections of Project Plan (AESP or UPP)


Refer to image above to view the various sections of Project Plan (AESP/UPP).



Sections of a Project Plan (AESP or UPP)
Let us look at the various sections of Project Plan (AES /UPP).

Sections	Details
Engagement Details	Engagement details consist of Engagement objective, scope, product vision and milestones
Project Process – Operational Process Definition (OPD)	OPD includes lifecycle phases, activities, reviews and testing activities and work products; covers application security aspects. (i) Engagement may choose one or more life cycle models depending on the customer expectations and scope of the work (Project Type) (ii) Every phase follows an ETVX structure:  Entry criteria, Tasks, Validation and Verification, Exit criteria


Sections of a Project Plan (AESP or UPP)
Section	Detail
Governance Plan	The Governance plan describes the organization structure, stakeholder matrix, communication plan and monitoring plan.
Workforce and Workplace Plan	The Workforce and Workplace Plan (Resource Plan) covers human resources, IT infrastructure and tools to deliver the project.
Quality Plan	The Quality plan describes the objectives, standards, metrics, process performance models, continuous improvement plan.
Risk Management Plan	The approach for managing the risks is outlined in the Risk Management Plan.


Sections of a Project Plan (AESP or UPP)
Section	Detail
Configuration Management Plan	Configuration Management plan describes: (i) Key configuration management activities including environment set-up (Development, Quality Assurance, Staging, Production and so on), (ii) Configuration management tools to be used - Configurable Items such as source-code, project documents for which detailed change history needs to be maintained, Access control requirements, Back-up requirements for configuration repository (iii) Continuous integration and deployment practices


Sections of Project Plan - Further Reading
For more details refer to the following.

Planning and Monitoring Progress Against Plan
Let us view a video on Project Initiation Activities.

When should you plan?

Planning is not a one-time activity, but a continuous activity.
Planning should start before the project activities begin
Plan should be revisited for updates
Every time there is a change in scope or timelines
On completion of milestone
Based on the progress of the project
For Agile projects

Release planning to be done based on the MVP/Release Goal
Iteration Planning for each Iteration of the release for the prioritized backlog items to produce a working product
Look-ahead Planning during execution of an iteration, for refining the Product Backlog to support the next 1-2 iterations and/or next release
How do you monitor progress against the plan?

Prepare/Update the Project Plan
Monitor the progress
Any deviation?
Identify corrective  action
Readjust the plan






Objectives
Let us begin with the third topic Estimation.

At the end of this topic, you will be able to:

State what is estimation and the need for it
List the components of estimation
Discuss TCS Estimation Models and the Estimation tool


Estimation Analogy
Let us look at a video on the estimation analogy.

Let us look at an Estimation Analogy between building a house and application development.

Building Development

The cost estimate of building a house will be the addition of the estimation of each of the sub-components of the house.
The user may add extra add-ons like security cameras, and a lift, which should be factored into the estimates.
Application Development

The total estimate to build the application system is the addition of Sub-System level estimates.
The non-functional requirements are considered as per the essentials such as security, performance and scalability on top of the estimation performed.


De-Coding Estimation
What is estimation?

Estimation is the practice and diligent work required to answer the question "What is the quantity of work?" before answering the question "How many people do we need to deliver the work?"
It quantifies work and provides the foundation for determining the effort, cost and time required to deliver the solution or services aiding in financial decisions and delivery commitments.
Why is estimation required?

Right estimation is required because of the following:

Promotes long term competitiveness and delivery certainty
Enables quantitative impact assessments and associated trade-offs
Provides the reference point for managing changes during execution
Promotes continuous learning and productivity improvements


Walking the Estimation Path - When
When should you estimate?

Estimation should be conducted during any of the following stages:

Proposal Stage
Contract Stage
Beginning of the engagement to validate the estimates
Product increment or Sprint planning
Changes to project scope


Walking the Estimation Path - What
In this video, you will learn about what should be estimated..

What should be estimated?

Size is the most significant component in estimation. It means quantifying the work for product or service using a standard method. Examples
Function Point (FP) for Development/Enhancement
Story Point (SP) for Agile Development
Number of tickets/transactions/calls for operations
Effort is another important estimation component, which has a direct impact on the cost required to deliver the work.
Effort = Size/Productivity
Productivity is the amount of work that can be delivered (size) per unit of effort (for example: Function Points delivered in 1 Person Hour)
Schedule is the duration required to complete the work. Schedule combined with effort helps in Resource loading.

Cost is the price for delivering the estimated size. It includes Manpower and Non-Manpower costs.


Walking the Estimation Path - How
How do you estimate?

You need to estimate each piece of work.

Use the available TCS estimation models or industry standard methods to estimate.
Estimate every change in scope and engage with your customer.
Track Estimates versus Actuals.
Monitor Baseline Productivity at regular intervals and use for future estimates.
Share your lessons for enriching organizational learning towards improving the estimation methods.


TCS Estimation Enablers – iEST Tool
Integrated Software Estimation Repository (iEST) is a central repository of standard estimation models. iEST enables you to:

Perform project estimations
Review estimations
Manage estimation versions
Perform early or quick estimation at the proposal stage
Perform detailed estimation during the various phases of the project


Estimation Enablers - TCS Standard Models


Here's a list of Estimation Enablers - TCS Standard Models.

Objectives
Let us begin with the third topic Risk Management.

At the end of this topic, you will be able to:

State what is risk and risk management
Recognize how risks are managed
Discuss what is Opportunity and Opportunity Management


Understand Risks
Risk is an uncertain event or a condition that may or may not happen. If it occurs, it has an adverse impact.

Risks are inherent to IT projects and IT operations. Some examples of risks are:

Late changes in requirements (risk) may result in delayed delivery (impact)
Unavailability of experienced skilled resources (risk) may result in poor quality deliverables (impact)
Failure of infrastructure and network (risk) may impact service continuity (impact)
Processing of personal information (PI) without a valid contractual agreement (risk) may result in a privacy violation (impact)
Cloud infrastructure without proper security controls in place (risk) may be vulnerable to external threats (impact)


Risk Management - Steps Involved


Effective Risk Management is about managing uncertainties in your project or business operations through proactive actions. It is done throughout the lifecycle to prevent the occurrence of risks and to reduce the undesirable impact.

There are 4 Steps in Risk Management.


Identify Risks
Let us view a video on risk identification.

Timely identification of risks and uncertainties is the key to effective Risk Management. Risks can be identified for both engagement and the projects under the engagement.
Leverage organizational level risk repository for risk identification.
While risks can be identified anytime throughout the project lifecycle, structured risk identification must occur during:
Review of the contract
Reviews like Project Startup (PSU), Project Management Review (PMR), and Audit
Sprint planning, execution and retrospective
Project technology reviews
Daily stand-up meetings or in weekly or monthly status calls
Structured Risk assessments
Privacy assessments
All the stakeholders cab stakeholders needs to be involved during risk identification
The identified risks from projects and the engagement should be recorded in the Engagement Risk Register
For each of the identified risks, a Risk Owner is assigned
The Risk Owner has the responsibility to manage the assigned risk
Example of Risk Identification : Delay in setup of code quality tools on Jenkins server that came up during daily standup call


Assess Risks
Risk owner will assess the identified risks for the following.

Loss due to Impact on quality, schedule, cost, effort, application performance, etc.
Probability of occurrence in view of the existing controls in place
Probability and Loss of a Risk occurrence determines its: Risk Exposure (RE) = Probability * Loss

RE is used to prioritize and plan Risk Response.
RE can be quantified into its Financial equivalent for better risk prioritization.
Example of Risk Assessment: Joan and Tim discuss about the chances of niche skill unavailability and its potential impact


Plan Risk Response - Reduce and Transfer
Risk Response involves identification of actions in the context of your project or engagement. A risk response can be either of the following:

Reduce: The risk is reduced by either reducing the likelihood of the risk or by reducing the impact of the risk. It is important to develop a mitigation and contingency plan.
Mitigation plan: Actions to be taken before the occurrence of a risk so as to reduce or eliminate the risk
Contingency plan: Actions to be taken when the risk occurs even after mitigation. Include a fallback plan in case the actions are not effective
Transfer: Risk impacting the project is transferred to another party
Develop contingency plan only.
Transfer to another party with full consent and acknowledgement.


Plan Risk Response - Accept and Avoid
Accept: Risk is accepted when options for mitigation, avoidance and transfer are not available.
Develop contingency plan only.
Approval by Delivery Partner for risks with high RE.
Avoid: When you have removed the source of risk itself (Risk is no longer applicable) it is termed as avoidance. Such risks should be retired.


Example of Risk Response Plan
Let us look at some examples of Risk Response Plan.

Training the programmers on how to handle common code violations and updating the coding checklist of the project is preparing the “Mitigation Plan” for the risk.
Presence of anti-virus tool in the laptop or desktop to prevent any cyber threat is an “Existing Control”.
Following pair programming and involving senior members and technical leads for peer reviews is the “Contingency Plan” in case the risk materializes.


Monitor Risks
Risk Monitoring is done by tracking, controlling, communicating and documenting the risks throughout the lifecycle of a project or engagement. Select play to learn more.

Monitor each risk for change in Probability or Loss. Monitor risks by: (i) Obtaining frequent feedback through iterations/sprints/team meetings or during PMR or Audit (ii) Collaborating within teams and with customers (iii) Conducting daily stand-up meeting and (iv) using visual information indicators
Analyze variation of RE over time to revise the Mitigation and Contingency Plans
Update the Risk Register as per the defined frequency in the project plan.
Retire the risks that are no longer relevant for the project or engagement.
Report the status of risks to the relevant stakeholders, include all top risks in periodic status reports.


What is an Opportunity?
This video explains what opportunity is.

Opportunity is an uncertain event or condition that results in a positive outcome on project or engagement objectives.

Some examples of opportunities are:

In the likelihood that the customer is willing to approve the budget for automation and tools deployment, project can deliver with faster release frequency
In the likelihood that the customer is agreeable to move from Waterfall to Agile methodology, entire engagement can leverage TCS Agile capabilities to deliver in quicker timelines
In the event that a higher version of a software is available, project may explore moving to the higher version <Python 2.X to Python 3.X> to achieve improved performance


Understanding Opportunity Management
Let us understand what Effective Opportunity Management is all about.

Opportunity Identification

Identify and document opportunities in the Opportunity register
Assign an owner for each opportunity
Opportunity Assessment

Assess Probability (P) and Benefit (B) (
Compute Opportunity Value (OV) = Probability of Opportunity occurrence * Benefit due to Opportunity realization
Rank the active opportunities based on the descending order of OV and then by descending order of benefit for prioritization


Understanding Opportunity Management
Let us continue with what is Effective Opportunity Management.

Opportunity Action Planning

Devise action plans to enhance the probability or benefit or both for the opportunity and ensure that the opportunity is realized
Opportunity Monitoring

Monitor each opportunity for change in Probability or Benefit as per the planned monitoring frequency
Analyze OV trend over time to understand the effectiveness of action plan and revise as required
Close opportunities that are materialized or no longer applicable


Opportunity Management - Further Reading
For more details refer to the following.

TCS-iQMS-058 Operational Risk Management Procedures Manual https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php/Operational_Risk_Management_Procedures_Manual
Risk Register template https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php/File:Org_it_(TCS-iQMS-058)_Risk_Register.xls





Introduction to Reviews and Testing
By the end of the fourth topic, Reviews and Testing, learners will able to:

State the importance of review and testing
List the different types of reviews
Identify the different techniques of review
Recognize the different types of Testing
State how to perform testing


Importance of Performing Reviews and Testing
Why should you perform Reviews and Testing?

It is important to conduct reviews and testing for the following reasons.

To deliver high quality products to users
To identify defects at an early stage
To avoid rework in later stages


Different Types of Reviews
Let us view a video on the different types of reviews.

Let us look at the different types of Reviews.

Self Review is done by the developer before giving it to any other person for review.
Peer Review is done by any other member of the project. 100% of the project work items must undergo a Peer Review.
SME Review is done by a Subject Matter Expert (SME) to bring an external perspective. Requirements Specifications, Architecture, and Design are recommended for an SME review. Code components must be sampled for an SME Review. Projects can define their own sampling criteria. Customer deliverables such as Software Requirements Specifications, User Manuals, and Online Help are reviewed by Documentation Champion as well.
Iteration Review/Product Demo is recommended for the Product at the end of an iteration or sprint. Review is done by the Product Owner/Customer through a demo given by the development team.
PI Review/Release Demo is recommended at the end of a release or Product Increment (PI), through a demo given by the development team. The Review is done by the Product Owner/Customer.








Techniques of Review
Let us look at the various techniques of review.

Walkthrough - Manual review where the reviewer reads the document or the source code to find errors.
Static Code Quality Analysis - This is an automated code review that checks compliance with standards. This is done using tools such as Sonar, Checkstyle, FindBugs, and PMD.
Pair Programming - One person writes code while the other observes and reviews each line of code while it is typed. This is an effective coding technique adopted in Agile methodology.
Static Application Security Analysis - This is an automated code review that identifies software vulnerabilities. There are tools like Fortify and Checkmarx for reviewing code security.
Note: Collaborative forms of reviews are also followed for critical items in a structured manner, where the author and reviewers jointly participate. A moderator may also be identified for such reviews.


Different Types of Testing - Functional

Functional requirements define the specific functions of a system. Functional testing is testing the application against the functional requirements. Functional testing includes the following:

Unit Testing
Integration Testing
System Testing
User Acceptance Testing
Regression testing


Different Types of Testing - Non-Functional

Non-functional requirements specify the operational capabilities of a system such as performance, security, accessibility, device compatibility and so on. Non-functional testing is testing the application against its non-functional requirements. Non-functional testing includes the following:

Performance Testing
Security Testing (Application, Network, and System)
Compatibility Testing
Portability Testing
Usability Testing
Accessibility Testing
Globalization and Localization Testing


Performing Testing
How do you perform Testing?

The following steps of test preparation, execution and closure are the same in all types of testing.

Prepare for Testing
Perform the Testing
Record Test Results
Verification and Re-Testing


Perform Testing - Prepare for Testing
How to prepare for testing?

Write Test Scenarios and the corresponding Test Cases.
Prepare Test Environment such as infrastructure, system software, data, software under test, and network.
Infrastructure setup may be automated.
Prepare Test Data that will satisfy all scenarios. Test data generation may be automated.


Perform, Record and Verify Re-test
This video demonstrates how to perform and record testing and verify re-testing.

How to perform and record testing?

Perform the Testing

Run each test case by performing the test steps.
Testing may be manual or automated through a tool.
Record Test Results

Compare the observed system behavior with expected behavior.
Record the observations and defects.
Record Pass/Fail for each Test case.
How to verify for re-test?

Fix the defects.
After the defects are fixed, rerun the failed test cases.
It is a good practice to run the full set of test cases for a clean run.
For Agile projects, some of the defects may be recorded in the Product Backlog to track and close in the future iterations.

Guidelines for Reviews and Testing (TCS-iQMS-106) https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php?title=Guidelines_For_Reviews_And_Testing

Objectives
By the end of the sixth topic, Measure to Manage Project, you will be able to:

State the purpose of Measurement
Define a Metric

Measurement and Metrics
Let us view a video on the purpose and importance of measurement and metrics.

What is the purpose of Measurement?

Lord Kelvin, Physicist had said that: When you can measure what you are speaking about, and can express it in numbers, you know something about it; … when you cannot express it in numbers, your knowledge is of a meager and unsatisfactory kind.

What is a Metric?

A metric is a measurement of a process, product or service attribute (cost, quality, schedule, customer experience, and business outcome). For example,

The ‘Number of bugs’ in software is a measurement of product quality
‘Effort Slippage’ is the measurement of effort accuracy (cost) of the estimation process Metrics are used to track, monitor and assess the success or failure of various processes, products and services.
Why should you Measure?

Peter Drucker said that What is measured, gets managed and improved.

Measurement helps you to:

Assess the project performance
Monitor the project progress
Compare with similar projects
Improve the product quality
Improve the delivery process


Collecting and Reporting Metrics
Let us view a video on how you should collect and report metrics.

All projects in TCS need to report metrics relevant to their project type and methodology. Metrics are reported in the Unified Metrics Program (UMP) in Ultimatix.

The Software Metrics Program (SMP) is an iQMS™ document that explains each of the metrics relevant for the different Project types. It is available in iQMS™ Wiki. For each metric, it explains the objective, applicability and usage.
The Metrics Deployment Plan (MDP) in iQMS™ is a listing of all the metrics for each Project type for easy reference. Based on the Project type and the type of work handled, you can choose the available mandatory and optional metrics.
The Domain specific Metrics Deployment Plan (MDP) for specific domains such as BPS, IS, Telecom, Cloud, IoT and Testing are also available as part of iQMS™.


Key Metrics for Transformation and Application Operations
Metric				What question does it answer					How do you use it				Definition
Schedule Slippage	What is the accuracy of schedule estimation?	Use this metric to improve accuracy of schedule estimation.	Defined as Percentage of schedule variance with respect to the planned schedule.
Effort Slippage		What is the accuracy of effort estimation?		Use this metric to improve the effort estimation accuracy.	Defined as Percentage of effort variance with respect to the planned effort.


Key Metrics for Transformation and Application Operations
Let us look at the key metrics for transformation and application operations - enhancement projects.

Metric						What question does it answer				How do you use it				Definition
Defect Free Delivery (DFD)	What is the quality of delivered product?	Use this metric to track how many high-quality deliveries are made to the customer.		Defined as Percentage of deliveries with no high severity (severity 1 or severity 2) defects in acceptance
On Time Delivery (OTD)		What is the timeliness of the deliveries made to the customer?		Use this metric to understand missed commitments.		Defined as Percentage of deliveries made “On Time” to customers.


Key Metrics for Transformation and Application Operations
Metric						What question does it answer								How do you use it			Definition
Review Effectiveness (RE)	What is the effectiveness of the internal review process?	Use this metric to evaluate the effectiveness of the review process. Defects should be detected as early as possible, as cost of fixing defects increases exponentially with every stage.	Defined as Percentage of defects detected in reviews with respect to the number of defects detected in reviews and testing.



Key Metrics for Transformation and Application Operations
Metric	What question does it answer	How do you use it	Definition
Escaped defects	What is the effectiveness of the defect detection process in the development life cycle?	(i) Escaped defects are measured to monitor the quality of the product deployed in production. It reflects on the quality of development and testing (ii) Use this metric to improve the testing and development process and the quality of the software delivered.	It is defined as Percentage of the defects that escaped into Production to the total defects detected in the product.


Key Metrics for Transformation and Application Operations
Metric	What question does it answer	How do you use it	Definition
SLA Parameters Met Compliance to Resolution SLA	To what extent are we able to meet service level commitments?	Use these metrics to improve service quality and customer satisfaction.	SLA Parameters Met is defined as Percentage of Contractual SLAs Met. Compliance to Resolution SLA is defined as Percentage of incidents resolved within SLA.
Reopened Incidents	What is the quality of the services rendered to the customer?	Use this metric to measure the effectiveness of incident resolution.	An incident is reopened if it is not resolved to the satisfaction of the end user. The metric is defined as Percentage of incidents reopened out of the total closed incidents.


Key Metrics for Transformation and Application Operations
Metric	What question does it answer	How do you use it	Definition
Velocity	What is the delivery rate of the team?	Use this metric to monitor the team productivity over a period of time.	Defined as Number of story points completed in every sprint. Size for the requirements is measured in story points in Agile projects.
Burn Down Chart	What is the progress of the team as per plan?	Use this metric to view the progress of the team with time and predict if the work can be completed in time.	Shows the amount of work remaining in the current sprint/ iteration in order to meet the Sprint goal.


Analyzing Metrics
Let us watch a video on how to analyze metrics.

How should you analyze metrics?

The metric data reported in the system should be analyzed by the project.

How does analysis of metrics help?

Analysis of metrics helps to:

Gain an understanding of past and present performance and predict future performance
Arrive at actionable findings which are useful for decision-making and improving the project performance
What are some commonly used techniques to analyze metrics?

Some commonly used techniques include Analysis against RAG (Red, Amber or Green), Bar Charts, Pie Charts, Histograms, Pareto Charts, Box and Whisker Plots, Scatter plots, Run charts, Regression Analysis and Control charts. (Please refer to SPC WBT iEvolve ID – 59340 for more details on these techniques.)

Analysis Against RAG is the basic process and is provided in the subsequent cards.


Analyzing Metrics
Let us find out more about how to analyze metrics.

How should you analyze metrics?

A metric may be marked as Red, Amber or Green in the system based on the target value set by the organization

Red indicates that the Metric value is not acceptable.
Amber indicates that the Metric value does not meet the target but is still acceptable.
Green indicates that the Metric value meets target.
Causal Analysis is mandatory for all Red and Amber metric values and action should be taken for improving performance.

For Example, for the metric SLA Compliance, the target is 99% and the acceptable level is 95%. If the SLA Compliance metric value for your project is 97%, it is in Amber band as the Amber target is >=95% and <99%. You should perform causal analysis in this case and take actions to bring it to the Green band (>=99%)
In case your project is exceeding the targets for a period of six months, share your best practices with other projects.

Introduction to Project Management Review (PMR)
Let us view an introductory video on Project Management Review (PMR) and the objectives of this topic.

In this topic you will get an overview of Project Management Review (PMR). By the end of this topic, you will be able to -

Discuss what is the objective of Project Management Review (PMR)
Explain what the inputs and outcomes of PMRs are
Describe what is the PMR process flow


Objectives of PMR


Let us view the objectives of Project Management Review (PMR).


Inputs for PMR
Some of the inputs for PMR are as follows.

Customer Sentiments and Feedback
Contractual Commitments
Delivery Timelines
Delivery and Service Quality
Financial Health
Risk Management
Resource Management
Future Plans


PRM - Risk based Rigor



Risk based rigor is needed for Project Management Review to identify and manage risks. Risk based rigor can be divided into 3 levels.


Outcomes of PMR
Let us look at theoutcomes of Project Management Review.

Early Identification of Issues/Risks
Contractual Commitment Adherence
Project health assessment
Customer Feedback is addressed
Drive continuous Improvement



PRM Process
Let us now view a video on PRM Process.



Let us view the Project Management Review process.

First, the reviewee creates a PMR presentation or KIT comprising engagement information that is to be shared with the Reviewers.
Then reviewee shares PMR presentation or KIT with the Reviewers.
The Reviewer then reviews the presentation and gets clarifications or additional information that is required.
The Reviewee, the Reviewer or the Unit DEG then conducts Project Management Review or PMR.
The Reviewer or the Unit DEG checks the RAG Assessment of the Engagement health.
The Reviewer or the Unit DEG then ensures or declares project coverage.
The Reviewer or the Unit DEG raises findings.
The Unit DEG prepares a Draft Report and sends it for review to Reviewee and Reviewers.
The Reviewer or the Reviewee provides feedback on the PMR report and ensures all issues are raised.
The Unit DEG finalizes the PMR report in the system.
The Reviewee ensures on time closure of findings raised in PMR.

Objectives
Let us begin with the eighth topic Process Performance Models .

At the end of this topic, you will be able to:

Describe what Process Performance Models are
List the different Process Performance Models


Process Performance Models – An Introduction
Let us view a video on Process Performance Models – An Introduction.

Process Performance Models

Process performance models are statistical models used to predict project outcomes.
By predicting future project outcomes, the project manager has better control over the project.
Using applicable models early in the project and using them throughout the lifecycle will help in effective planning and managing of the outcomes.
The models are built using performance data from past projects.
These models are maintained at the Organization level.
Example scenarios:

A defect prediction model may be used to predict the quality of the application being developed. Based on the prediction, the project processes may be updated to meet the desired quality level.
The planned Schedule of the project may be analyzed using a model to assess the schedule risks and refine the schedule if required.
Application Access:

Established models are available in COMPASS application in Ultimatix: Ultimatix > Applications > Delivery & Intellectual Property > Delivery Enablers > COMPASS Predictive Models


A Brief Description of the Models

Let us now look into a brief description of the different models, what each model predicts and a sample output.

CSI Prediction Model


CSI Prediction Model helps to predict the probable impact on Customer satisfaction based on improvement actions or current issues.
The sample output shows the probability of CSI increase for different ranges such as 0-5%, 5-10%, 10-15%..


Defect Prediction Model and Defect Injection Model


Defect Prediction Model and Defect Injection Model predicts defects across the lifecycle phases of development projects.
The sample output shows the predicted range of defects across project phases along with the actual counts.


Incident Arrival Rate Prediction Model

Incident Arrival Rate Prediction Modelpredicts the total number of incidents for production applications being supported.
The sample output shows the past and predicted incident arrival rate from an application being supported.



Schedule Analyzer Model
Schedule Analyzer Model
Schedule Analyzer Modelpredicts the overall schedule risk and identifies the task in the critical path.
The sample output shows the predicted range of the schedule and the impact of the tasks on the overall schedule variance.

Agile Sprint Planner Model
Agile Sprint Planner Model
Agile Sprint Planner Modelpredicts the completion probability of the planned stories within the sprints of Agile development projects.
The sample output shows the predicted range of effort for a sprint and the completion possibility of each story of the sprint.

Cost Optimizer Model
Cost Optimizer Model
Cost Optimizer Modelpredicts the cost impact of compressing a planned schedule and identifies the optimum schedule with minimum additional cost.
The sample output shows the Gantt charts of the original schedule and the optimized compressed schedule.

Generic Classifier Model (Bayesian)
Generic Classifier Model (Bayesian)
Generic Classifier Model (Bayesian)predicts an identified project parameter by learning from the past data of the influencing parameters.

The sample output shows the predicted range of the incident resolution time for various combinations of the input parameter values.

Cost of Quality Model
Cost of Quality Model
Cost of Quality Modelpredicts the monthly CoQ across SDLC phases of development projects.
The sample output shows phase-wise predicted range of CoQ components for a development project.








Objectives
Let us begin with the ninth topic Causal Analysis and Resolution.

At the end of this topic, you will be able to:

State the importance of Causal Analysis and Resolution (CAR)
Recognize when to perform CAR
List the CAR techniques
Explain how to perform CAR prevent defects


Understanding CAR
In this video, you will learn what Causal Analysis and Resolution (CAR) is,

Causal Analysis and Resolution (CAR) is a process that helps to -

Systematically identify and address the root cause of any defect or problem or any other negative or positive business outcome
Take corrective and preventive action to address the problem
Prevent recurring failures and support consistent success
Transform reactive to proactive culture
Reduce cost
Improve quality
What is a root cause?

A root cause is the underlying cause of a problem or any other negative or positive business outcome. In case of a negative outcome, once it is known and removed, the problem is permanently prevented.

What are the actions taken?

Actions are taken to address the root cause(s) of a problem, so that the problem or undesirable outcome does not recur. In case of a positive outcome, actions are taken to ensure recurrence.



When to Perform CAR

When should you do CAR?

Causal Analysis and Resolution is performed in the following scenarios:

For Negative Outcomes, when performance doesn’t meet the expectations/target. For example:

Customer Complaints
High severity defects reported in SIT/UAT
On Time Delivery is low
Repeated Incidents
Re-opened Incidents
Major Incidents
For Positive Outcomes, when performance consistently exceeds expectation and is significantly better than Organisation/Unit’s performance or the Project’s own past performance. For example:

An appreciation from customer
Customer Satisfaction Index is 100% in last 3 cycles
Defect free deliveries is 100% for the past one year
In Agile projects, causal analysis is performed during the retrospective meetings to arrive at the root causes and improvement actions


Causal Analysis Techniques










Causal analysis requires in-depth analysis for deriving the root causes of a negative or positive outcome. Multiple techniques can be used as mentioned below.


Causal Analysis Techniques
Technique Name	What it is
Brainstorming	Group or individual creativity technique by which ideas are generated to arrive at the root causes or actions
Nominal Group Technique (NGT)	Group process involving problem identification, solution generation and decision making
Pareto Analysis	Helps in identifying the ‘top few’ causes contributing to majority of the problems. Also known as ‘80/20’ rule, i.e., 20% of the causes contribute to 80% of problems
Fishbone Analysis	Ishikawa Diagram or Cause-Effect Diagram, to identify all the possible causes associated with a particular problem / effect
5 – Why Analysis	An iterative questioning technique to explore the cause-and-effect relationships and to determine the root cause of a problem. This is one of the simplest RCA techniques.


Preventing Defects - CAR
Let us view a video to understand how should CAR be done to prevent defects.

How should you do Causal Analysis and Resolution to prevent defects?

To prevent defects, identify the product quality related problem area for causal analysis. Examples: Software product quality related KPI, UAT Defect containment, Escaped Defect, Release Defect Density, or High Severity defects in all the stages. The technique used is Brainstorming.
To prevent defects, collect the underlying data that is, the defects. Examples: Collect details of the defects such as defect description, defect type, severity, or source. The technique employed is Brainstorming.
To prevent defects, find the probable causes for the defects and identify the top few causes. Examples: Assign causes like Incorrect Coding, Requirements Issue to each defect, and identify the top causes occurring across all defects. The technique employed is Pareto analysis.
To prevent defects, find the root causes that is the underlying cause(s) for the selected top causes. Examples: Competency issues and lack of unit testing automation for the defect cause ‘incorrect coding’ based on the specific project scenario. The techniques used are Fish bone diagram, 5-WHY, and/or Brainstorming.
To prevent defects, identify suitable actions to address the root cause(s) and implement them. Examples: Define how unit testing automation can be driven in the project. The technique employed is Brainstorming.
To prevent defects, evaluate the effectiveness of the actions taken. Examples: Perform hypothesis test on UAT defect containment pre and post the improvement action, to validate whether the improvement is statistically significant. The techniques used are Trend Analysis and Hypothesis testing.

Let us look at some reference documents now.

Project Procedure Manual: CAUSAL ANALYSIS (PP3090) https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php/PP3090
Project Procedure Manual: GUIDELINES AND TECHNIQUES FOR CAUSAL ANALYSIS (Appendix E) https://iqmskm.ultimatix.net/km/index.php/PPM_E

Objectives
By the end of the tenth topic, Software Configuration Management, you will be able to:

Identify the need of Software Configuration Management
State what Software Configuration Management is
List the key benefits of Software Configuration Management
Recognize the key activities in Configuration Management
Discuss how to use Traceability Matrix to assess a Change


Importance of Software Configuration Management
In this video, you will learn about the need for Software Configuration Management.

Why do we need Software Configuration Management?

Any software system consists of a large number of components such as files, database definitions, scripts, and documentation in the form of source code.
Such a system undergoes changes over time through regular maintenance and enhancements. These changes could be due to defect fixes or enhancements.
Consider a scenario when multiple developers are working on the same set of files or documents for the work assigned to them.

Their changes could conflict with each other, or the developers could overwrite each other’s work.

Such uncontrolled changes could affect the integrity and stability of the system.

Configuration management is a key discipline to ensure software stability and integrity in the midst of ongoing changes and software releases.


Understanding Software Configuration Management
What is Software Configuration Management?

Software Configuration management is the process:

by which all the items in a project are uniquely identified, modified, stored and retrieved
to manage, track and control changes to every single item of the software system
to prevent any uncontrolled change that could cause system failure or instability
The Configuration management strategy in your project will help you to:

determine how the items are identified
how changes are handled
how different versions of the software are managed


Software Configuration Management – Key Benefits
Configuration management practices help to -

Ensure that a well-organized system is in place, where developers can refer to the past versions of work items and recreate previous states of the software system and its environment
Make a change to any of the items and easily deploy the change to applicable environments – SIT, UAT or Production
Merge work of one developer with that of the other developers and fix conflicts, if any, when multiple developers are changing the same set of files
See each change made in any environment and trace it back to find out what the change was, when and why, and by whom, it was made
Prevent unauthorized access to all project items


Key Activities in Configuration Management


------------

Let us look at the four key activities in Configuration Management.

Identifying Configurable Items
Identifying Configurable Items refers to identifying the items of software system for tracking change.

All items in a software system for which changes need to be tracked and versions need to be maintained are called Configurable items.

Examples:

Source code
Configuration information for hardware, software
Executables
Data and data dictionaries
Test cases
Build and deployment Scripts
Project Plan
Requirement specification, Design document, Installation documentand so on


Software Baselining
Software Baselining refers to establishing and controlling the versions of software system.

A Software baseline is an approved stable state of a software system with a unique identifier that can be used for future reference.

Baselining of a software system is done at the end of each life cycle phase or at every major delivery/ release
The reviewed and tested version of a software along with all its components in a stable state is assigned a unique label for baselining. For example
For Example:

Version 1.0.0 for the first release
Next Major release will have the version number 2.0.0, next Minor release 2.1.0 and if the next release is a Patch release, the version number will be 2.1.1. This is called Semantic versioning.


Change Control
Change Control refers to controlling changes of the versioned items.

Change Control is a systematic approach for managing changes in a software system. It involves the following:

Raise the Change Request (CR)
Evaluate the CR
Obtain approvals for the CR based on the impact and effort
Develop and test program updates to address the CR
Implement the Change in the required environments
Note: For projects following Agile methodology, changes are addressed through new features and related user stories, which form part of the Product Backlog.


Configuration Control
Configuration Control refers to tracking changes of the versioned items .

Configuration Control is the ability to trace back to a previous version of any Configurable item.

Change history It involves the following: Maintain Configuration information of all items in a software system. Example: Version log must mention what is the change and why the change was made Maintain versions of items, use version control systems to identify, label and track their change status Maintain Baselines of these items helps to understand the change detail corresponding to the version.

It involves the following:

Maintain Configuration information of all items in a software system. Example: Version log must mention what is the change and why the change was made
Maintain versions of items, use version control systems to identify, label and track their change status
Maintain Baselines


Using Traceability Matrix to Assess a Change
This video will explain how to use the Traceability Matrix to assess a Change.

A Traceability Matrix is a crucial artifact that identifies the relationship among various components of a software system. It enables any software component to be traced back to the requirement.

Using a Traceability Matrix, you can identify all the impacted items while analyzing the changes to a requirement.

There are different types of Traceability Matrices that are used for impact analysis:

Horizontal Traceability Matrix helps to identify if a change in one requirement also impacts other requirements.
Vertical Traceability Matrix helps to trace corresponding items amongst the different work products throughout the software lifecycle.

Objectives
By the end of the eleventh topic Understanding Customer Feedback, you will be able to -

Recognize the relevance of Customer feedback and how it is obtained
Describe Customer Satisfaction Survey (CSS) steps in detail
State the importance of Customer Appreciation and Complaints


Customer feedback: Why and How
Customer feedback helps to -

Understand customer’s satisfaction with our products and services
Take actions to address any dissatisfaction
Understand the emerging customer needs
Identify areas of improvement in product and services
Customer feedback can be obtained through -

Customer Satisfaction Survey (CSS)
Appreciations
Complaints
Meetings with Customer

Customer Satisfaction Survey
In this video, we will learn more about Customer Satisfaction Survey.

Customer Satisfaction Survey (CSS) is an online survey to obtain structured feedback from customers on project performance.

The survey questionnaire consists of multiple questions covering different aspects of delivery, service quality, experience and business value. Questions also cover TCS ranking w.r.t other supplier(s) and customer’s willingness to recommend TCS.

Customer selects ratings and provides remarks against each question. Performance rating is selected on a 5-point scale: Strongly Agree, Agree, Neither Agree nor Disagree, Disagree and Strongly Disagree.


CSS Steps
Let us now view a video to understand CSS steps in detail.

Initiate and Collect Feedback - TCS will initiate a survey with the customer representative. Brief Customer on CSS process, project highlights and achievements, and actions are taken on previous feedback. The customer will then submit the survey and receives a copy of the feedback submitted.
Analyze Feedback - TCS will then analyze the customer feedback or response within 2 weeks. If CSS is low or there is a drop in CSS performance, TCS needs to analyze Opportunity for Improvement (OFI), Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI), Rank, Referencability, and Attributes.
Plan and Implement Actions - TCS then needs to review the action plan with key stakeholders, discuss the action plan with the customer and include if there are any inputs. TCS also needs to share progress on the action plan with the customer regularly.
Configure Survey - TCS or the Client will configure the survey. The frequency of configuring is as per important milestone or once in 6 months or once in 3 months for Agile projects or prior to the project end.


Customer Appreciation
Appreciation is a positive feedback from the customer on the quality of our products or services.

The customer may be satisfied or delighted with any of the areas – Project Management, Service Quality, Quality of Deliverables, Communication, Responsiveness, Infrastructure, Security and so on.

Once you receive a customer appreciation -

Record the appreciation in the system including details, nature and mode of receipt.
Analyze the appreciation and identify any Best Practices that are worth sharing with other projects and units.
Note: A simple ‘Thank You’ for work done, from the customer should not be considered as an appreciation.


Customer Complaints
Complaint is an adverse feedback from the customer on our products or services. Customer may be dissatisfied with Quality of deliverables, Skills & Resourcing, Communication, Project Management, Security, Service Quality, Responsiveness and so on.

Once you receive a customer complaint -

Acknowledge and record the complaint in the system and inform relevant stakeholders.
Address the complaint within agreed timelines, propose resolution to Customer, engage with relevant stakeholders and take actions to address the complaint.
Resolve and close complaint - Analyze and address the root cause of the complaint, Identify lessons learnt and improvement opportunities.
Note: Security related complaint should be handled in accordance with the TCS-iQMS-137 Security Incident Handling Guidelines.




Objectives
By the end of the twelfth topic Performance Improvement, you will be able to: Introduction to Performance Improvement (PI)

Define what PI Lifecycle is
Discuss deathon in TCS
State how competency in PI is built
List the PI Enablers

What is PI
Performance refers to the performance of any unit or area of work which could be a Team, Project, Customer Account, Business Process, Business Unit or enablement function.

Improvement could improve any aspect of the work such as quality, cost, speed and business value.

Performance Improvement is the practice of continuously improving the process, product or service aligned to business purpose.

Why PI?


-----------

Performance Improvement (PI) is needed for the customer, organization, enablement function and for YOU.

Performance Improvements Lifecycle – At a Glance
Let us take a quick glance at the Performance Improvements Lifecycle.

A sustained focus on continuous improvement that adds value to both the customers and TCS. The 6 stages of the performance improvement lifecycle comprise the following.

Identify opportunities
Prioritize opportunities
Analyze root cause
Pilot identified solutions - Based on the complexity of the problem to be solved , different PI methodologies like Plan-do-check-act cycle (PDCA), LEAN, Systematic Problem Solving (SPS), SIX SIGMA are followed.
Deploy successful solutions
Articulate and track benefits - Benefits achieved is quantified in terms of financial (tangible) benefits or non-financial (intangible) benefits.


Identify Opportunity through Ideathon
What is Ideathon?

Ideathon is a process to crowd source Ideas to solve a business problem. Qualified ideas could deliver exponential business value to our customers and TCS.

Why Ideathon ?

Generate Idea for identification of improvement opportunity and deliver business value
Generate Ideas to transform business process
Improve Business KPI and grow Business
Resolve business problem, technical or operational
Improve existing process or product
Generate Innovate Ideas to develop new products
Create a culture of proactive Ideation for business value generation in the organisation


Stages of Ideathon
Let us look at the 3 Stages of Ideathon.

Pre Ideathon - Planning and preparation of Ideathon
Ideathon - Executing Ideathon and evaluating the ideas that are generated
Post Ideathon - Demonstrating the Business Value delivered from the Idea
Useful Links for Reference on Training Materials and Nano-video : https://knowmax3.ultimatix.net/sites/peg-oi/pi/Ideation%20%20Ideathon/Forms/AllItems.aspx
